intro: April 1985. Pepsi is winning blind taste tests and it's getting embarrassing. Your researchers cooked up a sweeter formula that beats both Pepsi AND Coke in testing. You run Coca-Cola. You're about to change a 99-year-old recipe Americans treat as basically holy. What could go wrong (everything).

realOutcome.summary: New Coke was a legendary faceplant that the company somehow recovered from spectacularly. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: Blind tests say the sweeter formula wins. Replace the classic Coca-Cola recipe with 'New Coke' — or trust that people drink the brand and the FEELING, not just the sugar.

choices.text: Change the 99-year formula to New Coke
choices.reveal: Coca-Cola reformulated to New Coke in 1985 off taste tests. The tests measured sips, not loyalty — people didn't want a better cola, they wanted THEIR cola. Data said yes, humans said absolutely not.

choices.text: Keep the classic formula, out-market Pepsi
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You compete on brand and emotion, where Coke is undefeated, instead of on sweetness. No revolt, no crisis, just marketing that mogs.

choices.text: Launch a sweeter flavor NEXT to the original
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You add an option without killing the classic. You grab the sweet-tooth crowd AND keep the loyalists — the exact W New Coke fumbled.

decisions.scenario: To fully commit to New Coke, you'd pull original Coca-Cola off shelves entirely. There can only be one.

choices.reveal: Coca-Cola yanked the original. Instant outrage — protest groups formed, 400,000 angry calls and letters poured in, and people hoarded old Coke like it was gold. The ratio was nationwide.

choices.text: Sell New Coke alongside the original
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You never take the original away so there's nothing to riot about. Two products quietly split the market instead of one starting a rebellion.

choices.text: Test New Coke in a few cities first
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A limited rollout exposes the loyalty problem before it goes national. You catch the L in a test market instead of on every front page in America.

decisions.scenario: The backlash is a full national event. Talk shows, protests, one guy collecting signatures to bring back the original. Dig in and defend your decision, or reverse FAST?

choices.reveal: After just 79 days, Coca-Cola brought the original back as 'Coca-Cola Classic.' Front-page news, sales surged past pre-New-Coke levels, and the whole disaster flipped into a marketing gift. Redemption arc unlocked.

choices.text: Dig in and defend New Coke to the death
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You mistake stubbornness for strength. Every week you defend New Coke, customers defect to Pepsi and to their own freezers full of the old stuff. Malding publicly.

choices.text: Slowly phase the original back over a year
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A cautious slow-motion reversal robs you of the dramatic comeback moment. An apology in slow-mo hits different (worse).

decisions.scenario: The press wants to know what in the world happened. Own the whole mess publicly, spin it as secret 4D-chess genius, or say nothing.

choices.text: Own it: 'We're not that dumb, or that smart'
choices.reveal: President Donald Keough killed the conspiracy theories with the iconic line that Coke was neither smart enough nor dumb enough to have planned it. Honesty turned humiliation into affection. Rizz.

choices.text: Blame customers for not appreciating the new taste
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You tell your most loyal fans they have bad taste. Nothing revives a boycott faster than a brand that lectures the people it just betrayed. Bold strategy, Cotton.

choices.text: Stay silent and hope it blows over
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Silence lets the story write itself, and the story is 'Coke is out of touch.' A comeback needs a voice, not an exec hiding under the desk.

decisions.scenario: Crisis survived. Now the real question: what kind of company is Coca-Cola? Press your growing wins — Diet Coke, bottler consolidation — or retreat to just guarding the flagship.

choices.text: Press the Diet Coke win, consolidate the bottlers
choices.reveal: Goizueta pressed forward — Diet Coke became a monster hit, bottler consolidation fattened margins, and Coca-Cola grew from ~$4B to $145B on his watch. Turned the L into an empire.

choices.text: Retreat to protecting only the flagship Coke
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Shell-shocked by New Coke, you play defense forever. Flagship stays safe, and you miss the growth that made Coca-Cola a global juggernaut. Playing scared.

choices.text: Chase random diversification to feel safe
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You buy into businesses you don't understand instead of pressing the beverage empire you already dominate. Distraction cosplaying as prudence.

bands.line: You survived the blunder and grew an empire. Villain episode: WON. Clutch.
Bruised but recovered. Flagship safe, ambition not. Mid.
You broke the most trusted brand in the world. Faithful to history's worst instincts. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Coke turned its worst blunder into a comeback. Today's agents flagged a brand trusting the spreadsheet over its fans →
